interpretations.interpretation: As a straightforward genealogical record: the hearers would understand these as names of successive generations in a royal family line (Hezekiah → Manasseh → Amon → Josiah), meant to track ancestry and descent.

As a way to establish legitimacy and rightful succession: the audience could read the chain of parentage as grounding why a particular ruler (especially Josiah, the last named) had a recognized place within the ruling line of Judah.

As a demonstration of God’s providential ordering of history: the list may be taken as showing that generations rise and fall according to a divine plan, even when the narrative of those kings differs.

As a framing device for the larger narrative to come: listeners might expect that naming these kings in sequence signals that their reigns (and their influence on Israel/Judah) will be discussed, with Josiah introduced as a significant figure.

As a covenantal or theological marker: in a context where ancestry and divine promise are closely connected, the audience could see the genealogy as tying later events to earlier promises attached to the people of Judah.
